sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: An immunologist studies the response of a patient to a bacterial skin infection. She observes that within minutes of the breach, neutrophils migrate to the site via chemotaxis, engulf bacteria by phagocytosis, and kill them using reactive oxygen species. Macrophages arrive hours later and present bacterial peptides on MHC class II molecules to CD4⁺ T helper cells. Several days later, B cells activated by T-helper signals produce antibodies specific to bacterial surface antigens. Upon re-exposure to the same bacterium weeks later, the patient mounts a faster and stronger response.
questions.stem: The neutrophil response described — rapid, nonspecific, and not enhanced by prior exposure — is characteristic of:

choices.label: A
choices.text: innate immunity, because it provides an immediate, nonspecific first line of defense that does not improve with repeated exposure

choices.label: B
choices.text: adaptive immunity, because it involves antigen-specific receptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: passive immunity, because the patient received antibodies from another source

choices.label: D
choices.text: autoimmunity, because the immune system attacks self-tissues

explanations.B: Adaptive immunity uses antigen-specific receptors (TCR, BCR/antibody) generated by V(D)J recombination. Neutrophils use nonspecific PRRs, not antigen-specific receptors. The adaptive response (B and T cells) takes days to develop, not minutes.
explanations.A: Correct. Innate immunity is the rapid, nonspecific defense system that responds within minutes to hours. Components include physical barriers (skin, mucous membranes), phagocytes (neutrophils, macrophages), NK cells, complement, and inflammatory mediators. Innate responses recognize broad pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) via pattern recognition receptors (PRRs like toll-like receptors) and do not generate immunological memory — the response is identical upon re-exposure.
explanations.C: Passive immunity is the transfer of pre-formed antibodies from one individual to another (e.g., maternal IgG crossing the placenta, or therapeutic antibody infusions). The patient is generating their own immune response, which is active immunity.
explanations.D: Autoimmunity involves the immune system attacking self-tissues (e.g., type 1 diabetes, lupus, rheumatoid arthritis). The response described targets bacteria, not self-antigens.

questions.stem: Macrophages presenting bacterial peptides on MHC class II molecules to CD4⁺ T cells describes the process of:

choices.label: A
choices.text: antigen presentation, which bridges innate and adaptive immunity by activating T helper cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: antibody-mediated cytotoxicity, where antibodies kill infected cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: complement activation via the classical pathway

choices.label: D
choices.text: clonal deletion of self-reactive lymphocytes in the thymus

explanations.A: Correct. Antigen-presenting cells (APCs) — macrophages, dendritic cells, and B cells — process pathogen proteins into peptide fragments, load them onto MHC class II molecules, and present them to CD4⁺ T helper cells. This activates T helpers, which then provide signals (cytokines, CD40L) to activate B cells and CD8⁺ T cells. This process is the critical bridge between innate recognition (phagocytosis) and adaptive specificity (T cell activation).
explanations.B: Antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) involves NK cells killing antibody-coated target cells via Fc receptors. It does not describe MHC class II presentation to T helper cells.
explanations.C: The classical complement pathway is activated by antibody-antigen complexes (C1q binding IgG/IgM), not by MHC class II presentation. Complement can also be activated via alternative (spontaneous C3 hydrolysis) and lectin (mannose-binding lectin) pathways.
explanations.D: Clonal deletion (negative selection) occurs during T cell development in the thymus, where self-reactive T cells are eliminated by apoptosis. This is a tolerance mechanism, not antigen presentation to mature T cells during infection.

questions.stem: The faster and stronger response upon re-exposure to the same bacterium is due to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the innate immune system developing memory for the pathogen

choices.label: B
choices.text: pre-formed antibodies that persist permanently in the blood

choices.label: C
choices.text: immunological memory mediated by memory B and T cells that respond more rapidly and robustly upon re-encounter with the antigen

choices.label: D
choices.text: increased production of neutrophils in the bone marrow after the first infection

explanations.C: Correct. Immunological memory is the hallmark of adaptive immunity. During the primary response, clonal expansion produces effector cells (plasma cells, CTLs) and long-lived memory cells. Upon re-exposure, memory B cells rapidly differentiate into plasma cells producing high-affinity antibodies (due to prior somatic hypermutation and affinity maturation), and memory T cells mount a faster, stronger cellular response. This is the basis of vaccination.
explanations.A: The innate immune system does not develop classical immunological memory (though trained immunity via epigenetic changes in monocytes is a recent discovery, it is not the primary mechanism of enhanced secondary responses). Memory is a defining feature of adaptive immunity.
explanations.B: Serum antibody levels from the primary response decline over time — they do not persist permanently. The enhanced secondary response comes from memory B cells rapidly producing new antibodies, not from residual primary-response antibodies.
explanations.D: Neutrophil production is not specifically enhanced for a particular pathogen. Neutrophils are innate cells that respond nonspecifically. The faster secondary response is antigen-specific and mediated by adaptive memory cells.

choices.label: A
choices.text: all lymphocytes have identical antigen receptors and respond to all antigens equally

choices.label: B
choices.text: each lymphocyte bears a unique antigen receptor generated by V(D)J recombination, and antigen binding selects and expands specific clones

choices.label: C
choices.text: lymphocytes acquire antigen specificity only after encountering a pathogen

choices.label: D
choices.text: the immune system cannot distinguish self from non-self antigens

explanations.B: Correct. Clonal selection (Burnet, 1957) explains adaptive immune specificity: (1) each B or T cell bears a unique antigen receptor generated randomly by V(D)J recombination during development; (2) self-reactive clones are deleted (clonal deletion/negative selection); (3) when a foreign antigen enters, it binds and activates the specific lymphocyte clone(s) with complementary receptors; (4) the selected clone expands rapidly (clonal expansion) to produce effector and memory cells.
explanations.A: Each lymphocyte has a unique receptor — the diversity of the B cell repertoire is estimated at >10¹¹ different specificities. If all lymphocytes were identical, the immune system could not mount antigen-specific responses.
explanations.C: Lymphocytes generate their receptor diversity before antigen encounter, during development in the bone marrow (B cells) and thymus (T cells). Antigen selects pre-existing clones; it does not instruct receptor specificity.
explanations.D: The immune system distinguishes self from non-self through central tolerance (clonal deletion of self-reactive cells in thymus/bone marrow) and peripheral tolerance mechanisms (anergy, regulatory T cells, Fas-mediated apoptosis).

questions.stem: MHC class I molecules present intracellular (endogenous) peptides and are recognized by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: CD4⁺ T helper cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: neutrophils via toll-like receptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: B cells via their surface immunoglobulins

choices.label: D
choices.text: CD8⁺ cytotoxic T lymphocytes

explanations.B: Neutrophils recognize pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) through toll-like receptors (TLRs) and other PRRs. They do not recognize MHC-peptide complexes — that is the function of T lymphocytes.
explanations.A: CD4⁺ T helper cells recognize peptides presented on MHC class II molecules, not class I. MHC II is expressed primarily on professional APCs (dendritic cells, macrophages, B cells) and presents exogenous (extracellular) antigens.
explanations.C: B cells recognize intact (native) antigens through their B cell receptor (surface immunoglobulin), not processed peptides on MHC. B cells do not need MHC presentation to recognize antigen — they bind whole proteins, polysaccharides, or other molecules directly.
explanations.D: Correct. MHC class I molecules are expressed on virtually all nucleated cells and present peptides derived from intracellular proteins (including viral proteins in infected cells). CD8⁺ cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) recognize peptide-MHC I complexes through their T cell receptor (TCR). If the peptide is foreign (e.g., viral), the CTL kills the presenting cell via perforin/granzyme or Fas/FasL pathways. This is how the immune system eliminates virus-infected and tumor cells.

questions.topic: MHC Class I / CD8⁺ T Cells

choices.label: A
choices.text: macrophages after phagocytosing pathogens

choices.label: B
choices.text: CD8⁺ cytotoxic T cells after recognizing MHC class I

choices.label: C
choices.text: plasma cells, which are terminally differentiated B cells that secrete large quantities of antigen-specific antibody

choices.label: D
choices.text: natural killer (NK) cells in response to viral infection

explanations.C: Correct. B cells, upon activation by antigen and T helper cell signals (cytokines, CD40L-CD40 interaction), undergo clonal expansion, somatic hypermutation, class switching, and differentiation into plasma cells. Plasma cells are antibody-secreting factories that can produce thousands of antibody molecules per second. Some activated B cells instead become memory B cells, which persist long-term and provide rapid secondary responses.
explanations.A: Macrophages are phagocytes and antigen-presenting cells, not antibody producers. They engulf pathogens and present antigens to T cells, but they do not secrete immunoglobulins.
explanations.B: CD8⁺ cytotoxic T cells kill infected cells directly via perforin/granzyme. They do not produce antibodies. Antibody production is exclusively a B cell (plasma cell) function.
explanations.D: NK cells are innate lymphocytes that kill virus-infected and tumor cells without prior sensitization. They use perforin/granzyme and recognize cells lacking MHC class I (missing-self hypothesis). They do not produce antibodies.

questions.stem: IgE antibodies are primarily associated with:

choices.label: A
choices.text: complement fixation and opsonization of bacteria

choices.label: B
choices.text: allergic reactions and defense against parasitic worms — IgE binds mast cells via Fc receptors and triggers degranulation upon antigen cross-linking

choices.label: C
choices.text: mucosal immunity in the gut and respiratory tract

choices.label: D
choices.text: crossing the placenta to provide passive immunity to the fetus

explanations.B: Correct. IgE is the least abundant serum immunoglobulin but plays critical roles in type I hypersensitivity (allergic) reactions and anti-parasitic defense. IgE binds to high-affinity FcεRI receptors on mast cells and basophils. When antigen cross-links bound IgE, the mast cell degranulates, releasing histamine, leukotrienes, and prostaglandins — causing vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, and inflammation (allergic symptoms). In parasitic infections, IgE-mediated eosinophil activation helps kill helminths.
explanations.A: Complement fixation and opsonization are primarily functions of IgG and IgM. IgG is the most effective opsonin, and both IgG and IgM activate the classical complement pathway via C1q binding.
explanations.C: Mucosal immunity is primarily mediated by secretory IgA (sIgA), which is the most abundant antibody in mucosal secretions (saliva, tears, breast milk, gut secretions). IgA prevents pathogen adhesion to epithelial surfaces.
explanations.D: IgG is the only immunoglobulin class that crosses the placenta (via neonatal Fc receptor, FcRn) to provide passive immunity to the fetus. IgE does not cross the placenta.

questions.stem: The complement system enhances immune defense by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: directly producing antibodies that neutralize pathogens

choices.label: B
choices.text: promoting inflammation (C3a, C5a), opsonizing pathogens (C3b), and forming the membrane attack complex (MAC) that lyses target cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: presenting antigens on MHC class I molecules

choices.label: D
choices.text: activating only when antibodies are absent from the blood

explanations.B: Correct. Complement is a cascade of serum proteins with three activation pathways (classical, alternative, lectin) converging on C3 convertase. Products include: C3a/C5a (anaphylatoxins) — recruit and activate immune cells, promote inflammation; C3b — coats pathogen surfaces (opsonization), enhancing phagocytosis; C5b-C9 (MAC) — forms pores in pathogen membranes, causing osmotic lysis. Complement bridges innate and adaptive immunity.
explanations.A: Complement proteins are not antibodies. They are a separate system of ~30 serum proteins that enhance (complement) both innate and antibody-mediated immune functions. Antibodies are produced by plasma cells, not complement.
explanations.C: Antigen presentation on MHC class I is performed by the proteasome-TAP-MHC I pathway inside nucleated cells, not by complement proteins.
explanations.D: Complement can activate without antibodies — the alternative pathway is triggered by spontaneous C3 hydrolysis on microbial surfaces, and the lectin pathway by mannose-binding lectin recognizing pathogen carbohydrates. The classical pathway does require antibody (IgG/IgM) binding.

questions.stem: Helper T cells (CD4⁺) are critical for adaptive immunity because they:

choices.label: A
choices.text: directly kill virus-infected cells by releasing perforin

choices.label: B
choices.text: phagocytose bacteria and present antigens to B cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: secrete cytokines that activate B cells (promoting antibody class switching and affinity maturation) and enhance CD8⁺ T cell and macrophage function

choices.label: D
choices.text: produce IgM antibodies during the primary immune response

explanations.C: Correct. CD4⁺ T helper cells are the coordinators of adaptive immunity. Upon activation by APCs presenting antigen on MHC II, Th cells secrete cytokines: Th1 cells produce IFN-γ (activating macrophages and promoting cell-mediated immunity); Th2 cells produce IL-4, IL-5 (promoting B cell class switching to IgE and eosinophil activation); Tfh cells provide signals for germinal center B cell activation, somatic hypermutation, and affinity maturation. Without T helper function (as in HIV/AIDS), both humoral and cell-mediated immunity are severely impaired.
explanations.A: Direct killing of virus-infected cells via perforin and granzymes is the function of CD8⁺ cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) and NK cells, not CD4⁺ T helper cells.
explanations.B: Phagocytosis is performed by macrophages, neutrophils, and dendritic cells — not T helper cells. Macrophages present antigens to T cells (not vice versa). T helpers then provide activating signals back to macrophages and B cells.
explanations.D: IgM antibodies are produced by B cells (plasma cells), not T helper cells. T cells do not produce any immunoglobulins — antibody production is exclusively a B cell function.

choices.label: A
choices.text: providing a permanent supply of exogenous antibodies that protect against infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: suppressing the immune system to prevent autoimmune reactions

choices.label: C
choices.text: eliminating all bacteria from the body before infection can occur

choices.label: D
choices.text: exposing the immune system to a harmless form of a pathogen or its antigens, generating memory B and T cells that mount a rapid secondary response upon future exposure

explanations.D: Correct. Vaccines contain killed/inactivated pathogens, live attenuated organisms, purified antigens, or mRNA encoding antigens. These stimulate a primary adaptive immune response — producing effector cells (which clear the vaccine antigen) and long-lived memory cells. Upon subsequent exposure to the actual pathogen, memory B and T cells mount a rapid, high-affinity secondary response that prevents or minimizes disease. The key is immunological memory without the risks of natural infection.
explanations.A: Vaccines stimulate active immunity (the body produces its own antibodies and memory cells), not passive immunity (receiving pre-formed antibodies). Passive immunity provides immediate but temporary protection and does not generate memory.
explanations.B: Vaccines activate the immune system, not suppress it. Immunosuppression is used to treat autoimmune diseases and prevent transplant rejection — the opposite of vaccination.
explanations.C: Vaccines do not eliminate bacteria already present. They prime the adaptive immune system to recognize and rapidly respond to future encounters with a specific pathogen.
